16.1: Fearlessness, purity of mind, steady dedication to understanding, generosity, self-discipline, sincere offering, study of the Vedas (ancient teachings), inner discipline, and integrity are qualities that uplift human life.
16.2: Nonviolence, truthfulness, calmness, restraint from anger, renunciation of selfish motives, inner peace, absence of malice, compassion for all beings, freedom from greed, gentleness, modesty, and steadiness also belong to a noble nature.
16.3: Strength of character, forgiveness, patience, purity, freedom from hatred, and humility are marks of those whose nature is aligned with higher truth.
16.4: Hypocrisy, arrogance, pride, anger, harshness, and ignorance arise from a destructive mindset that leads one away from truth.
16.5: A noble nature leads toward liberation, while a destructive nature leads toward deeper bondage.
16.6: In human life two tendencies appear again and again: the tendency toward clarity and growth, and the tendency toward ignorance and destruction.
16.7: Those driven by ignorance cannot clearly distinguish what leads to growth and what leads to harm. They lack purity, discipline, and honesty.
16.8: Such people believe the world has no deeper foundation, no guiding intelligence, and that existence is driven only by desire and chance.
16.9: Holding such views, they become lost in destructive thinking and act in ways that harm both themselves and the world.
16.10: Driven by endless desire, pride, and delusion, they cling to false beliefs and pursue impure aims.
16.11: Their minds are consumed by endless anxieties and ambitions, believing that pleasure and possession are the highest goals of life.
16.12: Bound by countless desires and driven by anger and craving, they seek wealth and power through unjust means.
16.13: They think constantly about what they have gained and what they will gain next, believing ownership and control define success.
16.14: They imagine themselves powerful and invincible, believing that their strength and achievements place them above others.
16.15: Blinded by pride in wealth and status, they boast about their achievements while remaining unaware of deeper truth.
16.16: Lost in confusion and trapped in their own illusions, they become increasingly attached to pleasure and sink deeper into suffering.
16.17: Filled with ego and intoxicated by wealth and pride, they perform outward rituals only for display rather than for genuine understanding.
16.18: Driven by arrogance, desire, and anger, they lose awareness of the deeper reality that lives within themselves and within others.
16.21: Three forces lead human beings toward destruction: uncontrolled desire, anger, and greed. These should be abandoned.
16.22: One who frees the mind from these destructive impulses begins to walk the path toward the highest realization.
16.23: Ignoring the wisdom of sacred teachings and acting only from impulse prevents one from reaching fulfillment, happiness, or higher understanding.
16.24: Therefore the Vedas (ancient teachings) and wisdom traditions should guide one's understanding of what actions lead toward truth and what actions lead away from it.
17.2: Faith arises from one's inner nature and appears in three forms shaped by the qualities of Prakriti (nature): clarity, restlessness, and inertia.
17.3: A person's character is shaped by what they deeply believe. One becomes what one's faith is directed toward.
17.7: Even daily habits such as food, discipline, and generosity reflect the qualities that shape one's nature.
17.8: Foods that nourish life, clarity, strength, health, and joy are naturally preferred by those inclined toward sattva (clarity).
17.9: Foods that are excessively bitter, sour, salty, or harsh excite the senses and are preferred by those influenced by rajas (restless activity).
17.10: Food that is stale, lifeless, impure, or neglected appeals to those influenced by tamas (inertia and confusion).
17.11: An offering made sincerely as a duty, without expectation of reward, reflects sattva (clarity).
17.12: An offering performed for recognition, status, or personal gain reflects rajas (restless ambition).
17.13: An offering made carelessly, without understanding, respect, or sincerity reflects tamas (ignorance).
17.14: Respect toward wisdom, humility before teachers, purity, discipline, and nonviolence are forms of inner discipline expressed through the body.
17.15: Speech that is truthful, gentle, beneficial, and calm reflects discipline of the mind and respect for truth.
17.16: Calmness of mind, self-control, inner clarity, and sincerity of intention reflect discipline within the heart.
17.17: When discipline of body, speech, and mind is practiced with faith and without desire for reward, it expresses sattva (clarity).
17.18: When discipline is practiced for status, admiration, or recognition, it reflects rajas (restless ambition) and remains unstable.
17.19: When discipline becomes harsh self-punishment or is used to harm oneself or others, it reflects tamas (ignorance).
17.20: Giving generously at the right time, to the right purpose, without expectation of return, expresses sattva (clarity).
17.21: Giving with expectation of reward or recognition reflects rajas (restless ambition).
17.22: Giving carelessly, disrespectfully, or to harmful purposes reflects tamas (ignorance).
17.23: The sacred expression Om Tat Sat represents Brahman (the ultimate reality). These sounds have long symbolized truth, goodness, and alignment with the highest reality.
17.24: For this reason, acts of offering, generosity, and discipline traditionally begin with remembrance of Om, acknowledging Brahman (the ultimate reality).
17.25: When actions are performed without attachment to their results and dedicated to truth, they become part of the path toward liberation.
17.26: The word Sat refers to what is real, good, and aligned with truth.
17.27: Steadiness in actions of generosity, discipline, and offering that are dedicated to truth is also called Sat.
17.28: Actions, offerings, or disciplines performed without sincerity or faith lack alignment with truth and therefore bear no lasting value.
